metadata.title: Barrios Visibles | Argentina
metadata.description: Explore informal settlements in Argentina and compare official RENABAP data with satellite-derived building footprints.

stats.placeholder: Zoom to a settlement to view detailed statistics.

about.description: Barrios Visibles compares official census data from RENABAP with satellite-derived building footprints to reveal gaps in how Argentina counts its informal settlements.
about.credits: Built with data from Google-Microsoft-OSM Open Buildings and RENABAP.

step1.title: Global data, local impact
step1.description:
Census data age rapidly. Argentina's official records haven't been updated since 2023—but informal settlements grow every day.

What if satellite imagery could fill the gap?

step2.title: La Plata: The case study
step2.conclusion: That's 41,575 buildings unaccounted for—representing 137,000–229,000 people missing from official records.

step3.title: Barrio Sin Nombre: 12x undercount
step3.conclusion: Official data captures less than 10% of the structures in this single settlement.

step4.title: This pattern repeats nationwide
step4.conclusion: If the La Plata pattern holds, over a million families may be missing from Argentina's official records.

step5.title: Explore the data yourself
step5.dataSource: Building footprints from Google-Microsoft-OSM Open Buildings, hosted on

legend.info: This gradient shows the discrepancy between official RENABAP data and satellite-detected building footprints. Amber indicates areas where buildings significantly outnumber official records.

explainer.title: How This Works
explainer.intro: Barrios Visibles uses satellite imagery to estimate the gap between official census records and the actual number of families living in Argentina's informal settlements. The results suggest significant undercounting at the national level.

problem.title: The Census Gap
problem.p1: National censuses occur roughly every 10 years, but informal settlements grow faster than cities can count.
problem.p1cont: Urban populations in the Global South grow 4% annually; slum populations often outpace that rate.
problem.p2: Enumeration itself is difficult: informal settlements often lack addresses, maps, and street names. Residents may distrust enumerators due to fears of eviction.

findings.title: What the Data Shows
findings.stat1: More building footprints than RENABAP's official family estimate, based on spatial analysis of 6,467 settlements nationwide.
findings.stat2: People potentially missing from official records, using household sizes from barrios populares studies (3–4 persons/family).
findings.stat3: Of Argentina's population may live in informal settlements—6.0 to 8.1 million people.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.p1: We compare two datasets: RENABAP's official count of 1.24 million families across 6,467 barrios populares, and building footprints detected from satellite imagery by Google, Microsoft, and OpenStreetMap contributors.
methodology.p2: Building detection in informal areas has known limitations—validation studies show 27–56% overlap with ground truth,
methodology.p2cont: significantly lower than formal urban contexts. This means our building counts likely underestimate the true number of structures.

caveats.title: Important Caveats
caveats.item1: Buenos Aires (CABA) is structurally different: multi-story villas mean multiple families per building footprint. We apply a 2.7× correction factor there.
caveats.item2: Building footprints are a floor estimate, not a ceiling. In consolidated settlements with vertical densification, actual households exceed building counts.
caveats.item3: Satellite imagery date may differ from RENABAP survey date (projections from 2010 census, published 2023).

footnotes.title: Sources & References
footnotes.fn1: Census operations consume 10–15% of national statistical office budgets.
footnotes.fn2: Housing, Slums and Informal Settlements data portal.
footnotes.fn3: Spatial data challenges in informal settlements.
footnotes.fn5: Jaccard Index 0.27–0.56 for informal settlements; 0.68–0.84 for Global North cities.
footnotes.fn6: Vertical densification patterns in Latin American informal settlements.

data.title: Data Sources
data.intro: This project combines official government records with satellite-derived building footprints to compare what's counted versus what's visible from space.

buildings.title: Building Footprints
buildings.description: Machine learning models trained on satellite imagery detect building outlines worldwide. This combined dataset merges outputs from Google Open Buildings, Microsoft Building Footprints, and OpenStreetMap contributors, covering 33.8 million structures in Argentina.

renabap.title: RENABAP
renabap.description: The Registro Nacional de Barrios Populares is Argentina's official registry of informal settlements, maintained by the Ministry of Territorial Development and Habitat. It documents 6,467 barrios populares with an estimated 1.24 million families, based on projections from the 2010 census.

technical.title: Technical Details
technical.item2: Spatial join: Buildings intersecting settlement boundaries
technical.item3: Building data: Google-Microsoft-OSM Open Buildings via Source.Cooperative
